title: Opportunities
text:
Opportunities
We are looking for motivated students interested in fluid mechanics, turbulence, computational fluid dynamics, machine learning, and high-performance computing.
Students with experience or interest in programming, numerical methods, Linux, Python, C/C++, Fortran, MATLAB, or parallel computing are encouraged to reach out.

title: Selected Publications
text:
For the most up-to-date publication record, please also see Google Scholar.

Selected Publications


Gholami A, Mehrshad, C Santoni, S Shapour Miandouab, H Seyedzadeh, J Craig, and A Khosronejad. 2026. “Wind Farm Layout Optimization Using a Novel Machine Learning Approach.” Physics of Fluids 38 (5): 055124. https://doi.org/10.1063/5.0326424.


Santoni, C, A Khosronejad, X Yang, P Seiler, and F Sotiropoulos. 2023. “Coupling Turbulent Flow with Blade Aeroelastics and Control Modules in Large-Eddy Simulation of Utility-Scale Wind Turbines.” Physics of Fluids 35 (1): 015140. https://doi.org/10.1063/5.0135518.


Santoni, C, T Viren, L Shen, F Sotiropoulos, and A Khosronejad. 2025. “Large-Eddy Simulations of a Utility-Scale Offshore Wind Farm Under Neutral Atmospheric Conditions.” Physical Review Fluids 10 (5): 054801. https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevFluids.10.054801.


Santoni, C, D Zhang, Z Zhang, D Samaras, F Sotiropoulos, and A Khosronejad. 2024. “Toward Ultra-Efficient High-Fidelity Predictions of Wind Turbine Wakes: Augmenting the Accuracy of Engineering Models with Machine Learning.” Physics of Fluids 36 (6): 065159. https://doi.org/10.1063/5.0213321.


Santoni, C, Z Zhang, F Sotiropoulos, and A Khosronejad. 2023. “A Data-Driven Machine Learning Approach for Yaw Control Applications of Wind Farms.” Theoretical and Applied Mechanics Letters 13 (5): 100471. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.taml.2023.100471.

title: Facilities
text:
Facilities
This page will describe the lab’s computational resources, software tools, in-house codes, and research infrastructure.

title: Research Overview
text:
Focus
Our group uses physics-based modeling and data-driven methods to study complex flow systems across multiple scales. A central goal is to develop predictive tools that are accurate enough to capture important flow physics while remaining useful for engineering analysis, design, and decision support.

title: Research Overview
text:
Research Themes

High-fidelity simulation of turbulent flows
Wind energy, turbine wakes, and flow control
Machine learning for computational fluid dynamics
Environmental and atmospheric flows
Wildland fire and fire-atmosphere modeling
High-performance computing and in-house scientific software development

title: Contact
text:
Research Inquiries
Prospective students, collaborators, and research partners are welcome to contact Dr. Santoni regarding research opportunities in computational fluid dynamics, turbulence, high-performance computing, machine learning for flow modeling, wind energy, and environmental flow applications.
Please include a brief description of your background, research interests, and relevant experience when reaching out.

title: ACFE Lab
text:
About the Lab
The Advanced Computational Flow and Energy Lab’s research lies at the intersection of fluid mechanics, scientific computing, and data-driven modeling. We develop and apply computational tools to improve the understanding and prediction of complex flow systems in engineering, energy, and environmental applications.
Our research focuses on high-fidelity simulation, reduced-order modeling, machine learning for computational fluid dynamics, wind energy, atmospheric and environmental flows, and fire-atmosphere interactions.
Learn more about our research

title: People
text:
Principal Investigator





Christian Santoni, Ph.D.
Assistant Professor  Department of Mechanical Engineering  North Carolina A&T State University

 in LinkedIn 
 iD ORCID 
 G Scholar 


Dr. Santoni’s research focuses on computational fluid dynamics, large-eddy simulation, environmental and atmospheric flows, wind energy, machine learning, and high-performance computing. His work combines physics-based modeling, in-house code development, and data-driven approaches for complex flow and energy systems.
Research interests: computational fluid dynamics, turbulence, large-eddy simulation, wind energy, environmental flows, fire-atmosphere modeling, machine learning, and high-performance computing.

title: People
text:
Graduate Students





Ebenezer Ashimolowo
Ph.D. Student  Department of Mechanical Engineering  North Carolina A&T State University
Research area: Ebenezer is a graduate student in Mechanical Engineering. His research combines physics-based modeling, numerical simulation, CFD code development, and data analysis to investigate complex fluid flow phenomena across engineering and geophysical systems. He is particularly interested in leveraging advanced computational methods to improve understanding and prediction of multiscale flow processes.
Interests: Computational fluid dynamics (CFD), turbulence, wind energy, heat transfer, atmospheric modeling, and high-performance computing.

title: People
text:
Undergraduate Students





Xavier Shepard
Undergraduate Researcher  Department of Mechanical Engineering  North Carolina A&T State University
Xavier is a senior at North Carolina A&T State University pursuing a Bachelor of Science in Mechanical Engineering with a concentration in Aerospace Engineering. He is currently an undergraduate researcher in Dr. Santoni’s laboratory, where he is gaining experience in wind energy systems and wind turbine wake modeling using the FLORIS simulation framework developed by the National Renewable Energy Laboratory.
Interests: energy systems, advanced engineering and aerospace systems, materials science, nanotechnology, advanced manufacturing, engineering design, engineering management, interdisciplinary technology development, and emerging technologies.
